text: The surgeon removes tissue labeled 'A' from the paracervix, bladder, rectal pillar, and vagina. The resection occurs between the ureter and cervix, with the bladder and rectal pillar at the cervical level. The vaginal resection is less than ten millimeters, aiming to remove the cervix in two parts.

The surgeon performs a Type B hysterectomy, removing tissue at the level of the ureteric tunnel in the paracervix. The ureter is unroofed, and the bladder is positioned at a distance from the uterus. The vagina is separated by at least ten millimeters, with the neuronal part of the paracervix left untouched.
The surgeon performs an additional paracervical lymphadenectomy, followed by a resection of the paracervix at the level of the hypogastric vessel.

The surgeon transsects the paracervix at a lower level near the D-butane vein, with attention to the ureteric tunnel and hypogastric plexuses, indicating the type C-II approach in the nerve-sparing radical hysterectomy.

The surgeon performs a resection of the paracervix halfway between the ureter and cervix, with the bladder and rectal pillar positioned at the level of the cervix. The vaginal resection is less than ten millimeters. The cervix is removed in two parts, with the paracervix resected at the level of the ureteric tunnel after unroofing the ureter. The bladder and rectal pillar are positioned at a distance from the uterus, and the vaginal resection is at least ten millimeters, aiming to preserve the neuronal part of the paracervix.

The surgeon performs a resection of the paracervix at the level of the hypogastric vessel, removing the paracervical lymph node. The rectal and bladder pillars are addressed at the level of the bladder and rectum, with the vagina maintained at a minimum of twenty millimeters. This is a Type C-II procedure without nerve preservation.

The surgeon performs dissection in the paravesical space, dividing the uterine artery into paravesical space anteriorly and pararectal space posteriorly. The pararectal space is further divided into medial (Okabashi space) and lateral (Latsuko space) sections, highlighting the avascular spaces of the pelvis.

The surgeon navigates the paravesical and pararectal spaces, with the ureter visible at the promontory level, dividing the pararectal space into the Okabashi and Latsuko spaces. The dissection proceeds towards the second ureter, following the guidance of the Japanese Yabuki technique, focusing on the unroofing of the ureter within the ureteric tunnel.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device in one hand and a harmonic ultrasonic device in the other to perform the transection of the uterine pedicle. The advanced bipolar device, referred to as the in-sealer, is utilized for precise tissue dissection and sealing during the hysterectomy procedure.
